    * ( 0 )

    *     *     * 

Describes how the ancient Persian prophet Zarathustra descends from his solitude in the mountains to tell the world that God is dead and that the Superman, the human embodiment of divinity, is his successor. This title argues that the meaning of existence is not to be found in religious pieties or meek submission, but in a powerful life force. *

title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra Study Guide | Literature Guide | LitCharts
excerpts:
Section Title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra > ... > Extra Credit for *Thus Spoke Zarathustra*
Content:
Packing a Punch. Nietzsche ’ s style is unusual for philosophical writing, relying on aphorisms—short, punchy sayings that present general truths—rather than a strong narrative structure or consistent line of argument. Sometimes, the first part of an aphorism presents an idea and then undercuts that idea with an unexpected twist that elicits deeper reflection from the reader.
Musical Legacy. In 1896, German composer Richard Strauss wrote the tone poem Also sprach Zarathustra , based on Nietzsche’s novel. The piece’s opening fanfare is recognizable to many people because it was used in the film 2001: A Space Odyssey .

title: Menschliches, Allzumenschliches: Ein Buch Fuer Freie Geister by Nietzsche | Project Gutenberg
excerpts:
Section Title: Menschliches, Allzumenschliches: Ein Buch Fuer Freie Geister by Nietzsche
Content:
"Menschliches, Allzumenschliches: Ein Buch Fuer Freie Geister" by Friedrich Nietzsche is a philosophical work published in 1878. Breaking from his earlier essay style, Nietzsche presents hundreds of short aphorisms examining human nature, morality, metaphysics, and religious life. Written after his split with Wagner and inspired by French aphorists like Voltaire and La Rochefoucauld, the book challenges Christian morality and explores the origins of moral feelings. This marked Nietzsche's turn toward a looser, ... Read More more diverse philosophical style that would define his later works. (This is an automatically generated summary.) Show Less

title: The Death of God and the Desecration of Humanity - Breakpoint
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > [John Stonestreet](https://breakpoint.org/author/john-stonestreet/ "Posts by John Stonestre...
Content:
Subscribe On
[iTunes](https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/the-breakpoint-podcast/id324173353)
[Stitcher](https://www.stitcher.com/podcast/colson-center/breakpoint-commentaries)
[Spotify](https://open.spotify.com/show/5DREO891gAQMhm4Lkjx8Wd)
[RSS](http://colsoncenter.libsyn.com/rss)
Download/Print PDF
The German philosopher Friedrich ** ** Nietzsche was most famous for proclaiming, “God is dead.” He did so in two places. In  *Thus Spake Zarathustra* , Nietzsche promised humans could be the thriving successor, if they would move beyond religion and morality. In contrast, “ [The Parable of the Madman](https://www.historyguide.org/europe/madman.html) ” was more of a warning, written not to those who believed in God but to those who didn’t:
